mnemonics.title: 8 Primitive Types
mnemonics.content: BICS FVD - Byte Int Char Short Float Void(not) Double. Remember: 'Big Cats For Very Dangerous' - Byte, Int, Char, Short, Float, Void(not), Double.

mnemonics.title: Integer Types Size Order
mnemonics.content: B-S-I-L: Byte(8) < Short(16) < Int(32) < Long(64). Think: 'Bad Students In Low classes' - Byte, Short, Int, Long.

mnemonics.title: Floating Point Sizes
mnemonics.content: Float is 32-bit, Double is 64-bit. Remember: Double is bigger, so 'D' stands for Double = 64 bits.

mnemonics.title: Type Casting Direction
mnemonics.content: Widen-ing = Widen your horizon = smaller to bigger (safe). Narrow-ing = Narrow your focus = bigger to smaller (needs cast).

acronyms.usage: Executes bytecode and manages memory for primitives and objects

acronyms.expansion: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers 754
acronyms.usage: Standard for floating-point arithmetic used by float and double in Java

visualTips.visualization: Draw a stack diagram showing: byte takes 1 box, short takes 2 boxes, int takes 4 boxes, long takes 8 boxes, float takes 4 boxes, double takes 8 boxes, char takes 2 boxes, boolean takes 1 bit (conceptually).
Draw pyramid with byte/short/char at bottom (promoted to int), then long, then float, then double at top. Show arrows pointing up for automatic promotion.
Use a number line showing: byte: -128 to 127, short: -32K to 32K, int: -2B to 2B, long: -9Q to 9Q. Show exponential growth visually.

analogies.analogy: Primitive types are like cash in your wallet - you have the actual value. Reference types are like a bank card - it's just a pointer to where the money (object) is stored.

Widening is like pouring water from a small cup to a large cup - no spilling (data loss). Narrowing is pouring from a large cup to a small cup - you might spill (lose data), so you need to be careful.
It's like a group project where smaller roles (byte, short, char) automatically get promoted to the lead role (int) because the bigger types (int) are the default for calculations.
Think of char as a seat number in a theater. The seat number (char value) tells you where a character sits in the Unicode 'theater'.
